channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The Hard-Line Military Fraternity Running Iran
item.description: Decision making in Iran is guided by a small group of men associated with the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps.
item.dc:creator: Neil MacFarquhar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran showing the three supreme leaders, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomenei, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and his son, Mojtaba Khamenei.

item.title: Mira Nair Finds a New Audience as Mother of Zohran Mamdani
item.description: “I feel like we have given him to the world,” the filmmaker said in a conversation with The New York Times. Her next movie is about one of India’s greatest artists.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das
item.media:credit: Atul Loke for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mira Nair, the acclaimed film director and mother of Zohran Mamdani, in Amritsar, India, in March.

item.title: Timmy the Whale Got Stranded Off the German Coast. Then Things Got Weird.
item.description: A humpback that became stuck in the Baltic seemed to unite a nation in hope. Rescue efforts followed, and then finger-pointing.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley and Jenny Gross
item.media:description: The humpback whale, nicknamed Timmy, trapped near the island of Poel, Germany, in April.

item.title: Days After Hosting Trump, Xi Serves Up Tea and Smiles to Putin
item.description: During a state visit, the Chinese and Russian leaders cast themselves as a stabilizing force in a world thrown into tumult by Washington.
item.dc:creator: David Pierson and Paul Sonne
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia and the Chinese leader Xi Jinping inspecting an honor guard at the Great Hall of the People in Beijing on Wednesday.

item.title: Cuba Says the Trump Administration Is Not Negotiating in Good Faith
item.description: Cuba’s ambassador to the United Nations said in an interview that Havana wanted to talk but the Trump administration was creating pretexts for military action.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas
item.media:description: Ernesto Soberón Guzmán, Cuba’s ambassador to the U.N., in New York on Wednesday.

item.title: How Iran Gained Leverage in the War
item.description: Outmatched militarily, Iran used “triangular coercion” by attacking Gulf states and closing the Strait of Hormuz. It points to a long-term U.S. vulnerability.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Taub
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Enghelab Square in Tehran in April showing American military hardware trapped in a net.

item.title: Ebola Crisis Sparks Debate Over Global Health Double Standards
item.description: To some Africans, the claim that the continent’s largest health agency had already bungled its response scratched a familiar wound.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Red Cross workers carry the coffin of a person who died of Ebola at a health center in Rwampara, the Democratic Republic of Congo, on Wednesday.

item.title: The French Civil Servant Accused of Drugging More Than 100 Women
item.description: For years, prosecutors have accused Christian Nègre, once a senior official, of slipping diuretics into women’s drinks. Despite admitting to some accusations in an interview with a French daily, he is yet to stand trial.
item.dc:creator: Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:credit: Juliette Pavy for The New York Times
item.media:description: A path along the River Ill in Strasbourg, France, where Marie-Hélène Brice said Christian Nègre brought her during a job interview.

item.title: Ebola Outbreak Is Unlikely to Become Global Threat, W.H.O. Says
item.description: Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, the World Health Organization’s director general, said the outbreak in central Africa was “not a pandemic emergency.”
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel, Nick Cumming-Bruce and Ephrat Livni
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, the director-general of the World Health Organization, speaking in Geneva on Wednesday.

item.title: Video Shows Itamar Ben-Gvir Taunting Pro-Palestinian Activists
item.description: The national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, posted a video in which he is seen with handcuffed activists forced to kneel on the deck of a ship. It drew a rebuke from Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer
item.media:description: Israel’s national security minister, Itamar Ben Gvir, in the Israeli parliament, on Wednesday.

item.title: Killing in Japan Stirs Fears of Tokuryu Crime Rings
item.description: Four teenage boys were arrested in the killing of a woman at her home. The police say they might have been directed by a new type of transient criminal network known as tokuryu.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández and Kiuko Notoya
item.media:description: A view of the compound where a 69-year-old woman was killed in Kaminokawa, Japan, last week.

item.title: Iran Threatens to Strike Beyond the Middle East if the U.S. Resumes Attacks
item.description: The warning was issued as President Trump and Vice President JD Vance say progress is being made toward a deal, while keeping open the threat of renewed strikes.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward and Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps stand near a drone display at a government-organized march in Tehran last month.

item.title: China Confirms It Will Buy 200 Boeing Planes
item.description: Beijing’s acknowledgment on Wednesday that tariffs were discussed in talks with President Trump signals it was prepared to push back if the U.S. revives duties.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Catie Edmondson
item.media:description: President Trump attending a state banquet with China’s top leader, Xi Jinping, in Beijing last week.

item.title: Soccer Game Between North and South Korean Teams Draws Strong Emotion On and Off the Pitch
item.description: While diplomatic relations between the two Koreas are near a historic low, a rare trip by athletes from the North triggered intense emotions in some older South Koreans.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Chung Kyung-jo, center, waving, supported the visiting team in the match between two Korean teams, in Suwon, South Korea on Wednesday.

item.title: China’s Housing Slump Shows Signs of Bottoming Out. We’ve Been Here Before.
item.description: Property prices in Shanghai, in particular, are rebounding, but the national market still faces an enormous overhang — 90 million empty or unfinished apartments.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:description: Many families in China poured their savings into apartments once seen as a safe path to wealth, only to watch values unravel.

item.title: How China’s Housing Crisis Has Global Consequences
item.description: Our Beijing bureau chief, Keith Bradsher, describes how China’s enormous housing crash is affecting economies all over the world.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher, Rebecca Suner, Thomas Vollkommer, Joey Sendaydiego and Luke Piotrowski

item.title: News of U.S. Indictment Slow to Reach Cubans Waiting for a Breakthrough
item.description: While many Cubans were divided over the legitimacy of the U.S. charging Raul Castro with murder, the hope for developments that might ease their suffering is widespread. “This has to change.”
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola
item.media:credit: Lisette Poole González for The New York Times
item.media:description: A furniture workshop in Havana carries reminders that the Castro family has been part of Cuba’s ruling elite for more than 65 years.

item.title: Autocracy’s ‘Loyal Losers’
item.description: Even the most capable autocrats need help hanging on to power. Mediocre officials are often best for the job, according to new research.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold

item.title: Ebola Containment Efforts May Have Been Hindered by USAID Shutdown and CDC Cuts
item.description: Aid cuts by the Trump administration have shut down crucial disease surveillance networks and medical supply chains in East Africa.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Stephanie Nolen
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Jospin Mwisha/Agence France-Presse via Getty Images
item.media:description: Monitoring visitors in Goma, in the Democratic Republic of Congo, where Ebola cases have been confirmed, on Monday.

item.title: Rubio Moves Closer to Decades-Long Goal of Transforming Cuba
item.description: The secretary of state is the son of immigrants who left Cuba before the Castro revolution to look for economic opportunities. Yet, he pursues change with the zeal of a political exile.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: Marco Rubio, the secretary of state and a former senator, is a first-generation American citizen whose parents immigrated from Cuba.

item.title: Why the U.S. Chose May 20 to Seek Charges Against Raúl Castro
item.description: The choice of this specific date to indict the former president Raúl Castro carries historical echoes from more than a century ago.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak
item.media:description: A wall in Havana featuring political leaders including the former Cuban president Raúl Castro.

item.title: After Eurovision Final, Dismay That Politics Didn’t Play a Bigger Role
item.description: Politics isn’t supposed to influence the international song contest, but some people wanted relationships between countries to hold more sway in this year’s voting.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Martin Meissner/Associated Press, via Ap Photo/Martin Meissner
item.media:description: Alexandra Capitanescu representing Romania in the Eurovision Song Contest last week.

item.title: Justice Dept. Charges Raúl Castro as Trump Escalates Pressure Campaign Against Cuba
item.description: The indictment was an extraordinary escalation of the Trump administration’s multifaceted pressure campaign against Cuba’s Communist government.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer, Frances Robles and David C. Adams
item.media:description: The indictment of Raúl Castro, the former president of Cuba, comes as the country’s oil supplies for domestic use and power plants has been exhausted.

item.title: Australian Swimmer Who Crashed Beach Fashion Show Becomes the Star
item.description: David Handley was going for a swim in Sydney when he found himself walking on an Australian Fashion Week runway. “I’ve usurped the spot of the lead model,” he said.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai

item.title: Bodies of Last 2 Missing Italian Divers Recovered in the Maldives
item.description: Experts have spent days conducting a complex search operation to find five divers who died on a trip to explore deep underwater caves in the Indian Ocean last week.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Josephine de La Bruyère
item.media:description: Maldivian health workers carrying the recovered bodies of two Italian divers on Wednesday.

item.title: James Roscoe, Deputy British Ambassador to the U.S., Leaves Job Abruptly
item.description: James Roscoe, a diplomat who previously worked for the royal family and at the United Nations, left his role without an immediate explanation.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Chris Jackson/Chris Jackson Collection, via Getty Images
item.media:description: James Roscoe, second right, at Windsor Castle in England during President Trump’s state visit, in September.

item.title: Heavy Rain and Floods in China Kill at Least 22
item.description: China’s most intense storm this year set off damaging flash floods in seven provinces, forcing tens of thousands of people to evacuate their homes.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:description: Floods after heavy rain in Duyun, in China’s southwestern Guizhou province, on Tuesday.

item.title: Early War Goal Was to Install Hard-Line Former President as Iran’s Leader
item.description: An Israeli strike designed to free Mahmoud Ahmadinejad from house arrest in Tehran, U.S. officials said, was part of an effort to bring about regime change and put him in power.
item.dc:creator: Mark Mazzetti, Julian E. Barnes, Farnaz Fassihi and Ronen Bergman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Former President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad of Iran registering as a candidate in the presidential election in Tehran in 2024.

item.title: What the End of Aid Looks Like
item.description: The United States and other countries are cutting humanitarian relief. Our reporter went to Somalia to see the impact.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Finbarr O'Reilly for The New York Times

item.title: The New ‘Gold Rush’ of Geothermal Energy
item.description: A new wave of start-ups are trying to harvest emissions-free energy from inside the Earth, but the industry still faces significant challenges.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:description: Executives of Fervo and others celebrated the geothermal company’s I.P.O. on the Nasdaq on May 13.

item.title: Bolivia Has Been Paralyzed by Protests. Here’s Why.
item.description: After two decades of leftist rule, many voters who backed Bolivia’s new, more conservative president say he’s made their lives harder and are demanding his removal.
item.dc:creator: Genevieve Glatsky and María Silvia Trigo
item.media:description: Demonstrators amid tear gas, calling for the resignation of President Rodrigo Paz, in La Paz, Bolivia, on Monday.

item.title: Israeli Minister Threatens to Evict Palestinians From West Bank Hamlet
item.description: Bezalel Smotrich, the hard-line minister, said he was retaliating for the International Criminal Court prosecutor seeking his arrest, something the court did not confirm nor deny.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman
item.media:description: The Israeli finance minister, Bezalel Smotrich, in Jerusalem earlier this month.

item.title: Zapatero, Spain’s Former Leader, Investigated for Graft
item.description: José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero, who led Spain from 2004 to 2011, is accused of influence peddling after he left office. He has denied wrongdoing.
item.dc:creator: Carlos Barragán
item.media:credit: Oscar Del Pozo/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero, a former prime minister of Spain, in Valencia last year.

item.title: ‘Married at First Sight’ Rape Allegations Lead to Call for U.K. Investigation
item.description: Two women told the BBC that they were sexually assaulted during the filming of the popular show. The accusations have reignited a debate in Britain about the ethics of reality TV.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.category: Married at First Sight UK (TV Program)
item.media:description: The British broadcaster Channel 4 removed episodes of the reality television show “Married at First Sight UK” after allegations of rape and sexual misconduct during filming.

item.title: Son of Mango Fashion Chain’s Founder Is Arrested as Suspect in His Death
item.description: Isak Andic, the billionaire who built the Spanish brand, died while hiking with his son in 2024. News outlets had reported that the son was under investigation.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Carlos Barragán
item.media:description: Jonathan Andic being escorted to court in Martorell, Spain, near Barcelona, on Tuesday.

item.title: Inflation Fears Cloud G7 Economic Agenda as Iran War Persists
item.description: The United States and Europe were at odds over the Trump administration’s decision to ease oil sanctions on Russia.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: As a Weakened Putin Follows Trump to Beijing, Iran War Offers an Opening
item.description: With the upheaval in the Persian Gulf disrupting oil and gas supplies, Russia is looking to deepen its energy ties to China.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko and David Pierson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Vladimir Smirnov/Sputnik, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: In a photo released by Russian state media, President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia was met by China’s foreign minister, Wang Yi, after arriving in Beijing on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump’s Special Envoy to Greenland Receives a Cold Welcome From Locals
item.description: After President Trump’s threats to seize the island, Gov. Jeff Landry’s offers of MAGA hats and chocolate chip cookies fall flat.
item.dc:creator: Maya Tekeli and Jeffrey Gettleman
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Gov. Jeff Landry of Louisiana at the White House last year. He is visiting Greenland this week as President Trump’s special envoy to the Danish territory.

item.title: Neanderthal Dentistry, and the Scientist Glad Not to Have Experienced It
item.description: The prehistoric hominins “apparently were very adept at what we would consider invasive medicine,” said the anthropologist John Olsen.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz

item.title: Starbucks Korea CEO Fired Over ‘Tank Day’ Ad Evoking Country’s Brutal History
item.description: A marketing campaign called “Tank Day” coincided with the anniversary of a military dictatorship’s crackdown on pro-democracy demonstrators.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:description: A Starbucks in Seoul, last week.

item.title: Trump Says He Postponed a ‘Very Major Attack’ on Iran, Leaving Mideast on Edge
item.description: President Trump threatened “another big hit” on Iran, saying the country had days to return to the negotiating table, as Pakistan continued its mediation efforts to end the war.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward and Elian Peltier
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump said the U.S. military was prepared for a “full, large scale assault” against Iran.

item.title: Ebola Outbreak in Central Africa Could Last Months, W.H.O. Says
item.description: Health officials reported more than 130 suspected deaths and 540 cases in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda, but experts say the figures could be much higher.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel, Yan Zhuang and Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: A visitor has their temperature checked before entering Kyeshero Hospital in Goma on Monday.

item.title: As Ukraine Strikes Russian Oil Sites, an Environmental Disaster Unfolds
item.description: After invading more than four years ago, Moscow has usually been the one causing ecological disaster. But Kyiv’s strikes, intended to cut into the Kremlin’s oil revenue, have flipped the script.
item.dc:creator: Nataliya Vasilyeva and Alina Lobzina
item.media:description: A satellite image from the aerial mapping company Vantor of a Russian oil facility in Tuapse, Russia, after Ukrainian drone attacks in April.

item.title: Trump’s Portrait Pops Up on Rickshaws in India to Mark America’s 250th
item.description: The president is popping up on the back of the iconic three-wheeler to kick off America’s 250th anniversary celebrations in India.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli
item.media:credit: Anindito Mukherjee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Md. Tayemkhan, a rickshaw driver, waited for hours near the U.S. Embassy in New Delhi to get a banner featuring President Donald Trump put on the back of his vehicle. He said he was paid about $16.

item.title: Oil Slick Reaches a Pristine Persian Gulf Island in Iran
item.description: Videos show birds, turtles and crabs trapped inside mounds of tar around Shidvar island, a protected wildlife sanctuary with turquoise waters and white sand beaches.
item.dc:creator: Sanam Mahoozi, Erika Solomon and Devon Lum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A video verified by The Times shows oil coating the shoreline of Shidvar Island as smoke rises from the Lavan refinery, seen in the distance.

item.title: Nauru Hopes You Will Call It by a New Name
item.description: Tiny Nauru could soon be called Naoero.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather

item.title: Here’s How Iran Could Respond to Renewed U.S.-Israeli Strikes
item.description: In any new round of fighting, Iranian officials could adopt new tactics, including intensified strikes on neighbors and trying to close off a second strait.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Iranians have used their proximity to the Strait of Hormuz, a critical trade waterway, to exert immense pressure on the global economy.

item.title: U.S. Hits Top Cuban Officials With Sanctions
item.description: The Trump administration, ratcheting up its effort to cripple the Cuban government, also imposed sanctions on three government agencies, including the police.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:description: The Cuban capital this week.

item.title: Étienne Davignon Dies Before Trial in Patrice Lumumba Killing
item.description: Étienne Davignon had been set to stand trial in connection with the murder of Patrice Lumumba, the first prime minister of what is now the Democratic Republic of Congo.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Benoit Doppagne/BELGA, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Étienne Davignon in 2018.

item.title: South Korea and Japan Leaders’ Summit: What to Know
item.description: Japan’s prime minister, Sanae Takaichi, and South Korea’s president, Lee Jae Myung, have surprised many observers by moving past their nations’ historical grievances.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:description: Japan’s prime minister, Sanae Takaichi, and South Korea’s president, Lee Jae Myung, met in Andong, South Korea, on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump Threatens Iran and Then Pulls Back, All in the Same Day
item.description: President Trump has repeatedly said he’ll restart military action against Iran, only to stop short of plunging the United States directly back into an unpopular war.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A U.S. military official said that Iran had been able to study the flight patterns of American fighter jets and bombers.

item.title: When Nationalism Beats Nationalism
item.description: A progressive, pluralistic nationalism has prevailed at the polls in Scotland and Wales. Could it gain traction elsewhere?
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold

item.title: Scientists Tweaked the Global Warming Outlook. So Trump Weighed In.
item.description: Renewable energy has helped make the worst-case scenario a bit less bad. The president said, falsely, it shows that climate scientists were wrong all along.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:description: Solar panels and wind turbines in north-central China. Renewable is becoming more affordable in many countries.

item.title: Ebola Was Identified in Congo Weeks Before W.H.O. Declared an Emergency
item.description: Early surveillance and testing failed to identify the rare species of Ebola responsible for the current outbreak. An American doctor is among the confirmed cases.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:description: At a hospital in Ituri Province, Democratic Republic of Congo, the center of a new Ebola outbreak.

item.title: Russia’s New Human Rights Commissioner Accused of Helping Kidnap Ukrainian Children
item.description: Yana Lantratova was instrumental in helping the chairman of her party to illegally adopt an infant girl from Russian-occupied Ukraine, the Ukrainian authorities say.
item.dc:creator: Nataliya Vasilyeva
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Yana Lantratova, in Moscow in October, will report directly to President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia.

item.title: Shakira Is Found Not Guilty of Tax Fraud in Spain
item.description: A Spanish court ruled that the pop star was not a tax resident of Spain in 2011 and ordered the country’s tax authorities to return tens of millions of dollars to her.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses and Carlos Barragán
item.media:description: The pop star Shakira has been embroiled in a yearslong tax battle with the Spanish authorities.
